text: This is 6 Minute English from BBC Learning English.
zh: [0] 这里是BBC英语教学的《六分钟英语》节目。

text: I'm Phil. And I'm Becca. 6 Minute English listeners are truly global, coming from all over the world,
zh: [1] 我是Phil。我是Becca。《六分钟英语》的听众真的来自世界各地,

text: including some of the coldest countries on Earth – places like Finland, where winter temperatures drop to minus 20 degrees Celsius.
zh: [2] 包括一些地球上最寒冷的国家,比如芬兰这样的地方,那里冬天气温可以降到零下20摄氏度。

text: Are you good at dealing with the cold, Phil? I'm not sure, but I do know that I don't like it.
zh: [3] Phil,你擅长应对寒冷吗?我不确定,但我知道我不喜欢冷。

text: What about you, Becca? Well, I'd rather be too hot and cool down than too cold and try to warm up.
zh: [4] 那你呢,Becca?嗯,我宁愿太热然后降温,也不想太冷再想办法暖和起来。

text: Yes, me too. Here in the UK, it never gets as cold as Finland,
zh: [5] 是的,我也是。在英国,天气从来没有芬兰那么冷,

text: but it's not unusual to see some people dressed in t -shirts while others are wrapped up in warm clothes.
zh: [6] 但看到有些人穿着T恤,而另一些人却裹得严严实实,这并不奇怪。

text: Why do people feel the cold so differently? That's what we'll be discussing in this episode,
zh: [7] 为什么人们对寒冷的感受如此不同呢?这就是我们这期节目要讨论的内容,

text: as well as learning some useful new words and phrases.
zh: [8] 我们还会学习一些实用的新单词和短语。

text: And as always, you'll find all the vocabulary from this episode plus a quiz and worksheet on our website, bbclearningenglish .com.
zh: [9] 一如既往,你可以在我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上找到本期的所有词汇,以及测验和练习题。

text: But now I have a question for you, Becca. According to the Guinness Book of World Records,
zh: [10] 不过现在我有个问题要问你,Becca。根据《吉尼斯世界纪录》,

text: the lowest temperature ever recorded was a very cold minus 89 degrees Celsius.
zh: [11] 有史以来记录到的最低气温是极冷的零下89摄氏度。

text: But where? Was it A, the Arctic? B, Antarctica or C the top of Mount Everest?
zh: [12] 但那是在哪里呢?A,北极?B,南极洲?还是C,珠穆朗玛峰顶?

text: Mmm, I'm going to guess B, Antarctica.
zh: [13] 嗯,我猜是B,南极洲。

text: OK, well we'll find out the answer at the end of the programme.
zh: [14] 好的,我们会在节目最后揭晓答案。

text: Our experience of cold depends on many things, including our genes, culture and place of birth.
zh: [15] 我们对寒冷的感受取决于很多因素,包括基因、文化和出生地。

text: But perhaps the most obvious thing is the clothes we wear.
zh: [16] 但也许最明显的因素是我们穿的衣服。

text: Professor Gnid Sartren lives on the icy island of Svalbard, 800 miles inside the Arctic Circle.
zh: [17] Gnid Sartren教授住在斯瓦尔巴德群岛上,那里冰天雪地,位于北极圈内800英里处。

text: Here she advises presenter Caroline Steele
zh: [18] 在这里,她为主持人Caroline Steele提供建议,

text: on how to dress against the cold for BBC World Service programme CrowdScience.
zh: [19] 教她如何在BBC世界服务频道节目《CrowdScience》中穿衣御寒。

text: So what should we consider when choosing our clothes?
zh: [20] 那么我们在选择衣服时应该考虑什么呢?

text: First and foremost, I would say that perhaps the gender differs.
zh: [21] 首先,我想说,性别可能是不同的因素。

text: So, male often are more tolerant perhaps than females.
zh: [22] 所以,男性通常可能比女性更耐寒一些。

text: Then it's of course what you're used to. Are you used to dealing with the cold? Are you not?
zh: [23] 然后当然还有你习不习惯的问题。你习惯应对寒冷吗?还是不习惯?

text: Gunhild says that first and foremost, men and women filled the cold differently.
zh: [24] Gunhild说,首先也是最重要的一点是,男性和女性对寒冷的感受不同。

text: She uses the phrase first and foremost to mean more than anything else.
zh: [25] 她使用“first and foremost”这个短语,意思是“最重要的是”。

text: She wants to emphasise that something, in this case someone's gender, is the most important thing to consider.
zh: [26] 她想强调,在这个例子中,性别是需要考虑的最重要因素。

text: There is some debate about whether men or women are more tolerant of the cold.
zh: [27] 关于男性和女性谁更耐寒,存在一些争议。

text: Being tolerant of something means being able to endure it without getting hurt.
zh: [28] 对某事有耐受力,意味着能够忍受它而不受伤害。

text: Another important factor is whether you are used to the cold, if being in cold environments is something you're familiar with.
zh: [29] 另一个重要因素是你是否习惯寒冷,也就是说,身处寒冷环境是否是你熟悉的事情。

text: It might sound obvious that someone born in Arctic Svalbard would feel less cold than someone born in Brazil.
zh: [30] 出生在北极斯瓦尔巴德的人可能比出生在巴西的人更不怕冷,这听起来似乎很明显。

text: But apart from environmental factors, are there actual physical differences that allow people to cope better with the cold?
zh: [31] 但除了环境因素之外,是否真的存在生理上的差异,让人们更能应对寒冷呢?

text: Dr. Kara Ockerbock studies reindeer herders in northern Finland,
zh: [32] Kara Ockerbock博士研究芬兰北部的驯鹿放牧人,

text: people who live in cold temperatures every day and have done for centuries.
zh: [33] 这些人每天都生活在寒冷的气温中,而且世世代代都是如此。

text: She measures their reaction to extreme cold and compares it to ordinary Finns from warmer parts of the country.
zh: [34] 她测量他们对极端寒冷的反应,并将其与来自该国较温暖地区的普通芬兰人进行比较。

text: Here, Kara shares her findings with Caroline Still from BBC World Service's Crowd Science.
zh: [35] 在这里,Kara与BBC世界服务频道《CrowdScience》的Caroline Steele分享了她的研究发现。

text: OK, and my guess is the reindeer herders deal better in the cold?
zh: [36] 好的,我猜驯鹿放牧人更能应对寒冷?

text: Yes and no! The more data we collect within this area, the more confusing the picture gets.
zh: [37] 是也不是!我们在这个领域收集的数据越多,情况就变得越令人困惑。

text: I can say that subjectively, at this point,
zh: [38] 我可以说,从主观角度来看,目前为止,

text: the reindeer herders at least kind of mentally handle the cold far better. They are far less likely to shiver.
zh: [39] 驯鹿放牧人至少在心理上更能应对寒冷。他们打寒战的可能性要小得多。

text: So, do the reindeer herders deal better with the cold? The answer is yes and no.
zh: [40] 那么,驯鹿放牧人是否更能应对寒冷呢?答案是“是也不是”。

text: A phrase meaning partly and partly not, used when there's no clear answer to a question.
zh: [41] 这个短语的意思是“部分是,部分不是”,用于当一个问题没有明确答案时。

text: However, Kara does say, subjectively, the herders manage better.
zh: [42] 不过,Kara确实说,从主观上讲,牧民们应对得更好。

text: They don't feel so cold. Subjectively means based on your personal inner experience,
zh: [43] 他们没有感觉那么冷。“主观地”意味着基于你的个人内在体验,

text: rather than objective facts. And physically, there are differences too.
zh: [44] 而不是客观事实。而且在生理上,也确实存在差异。

text: Reindeer herders are less likely to shiver – the shaky movement that cold muscles make to try and warm them up.
zh: [45] 驯鹿放牧人打寒战的可能性更小——寒战是寒冷的肌肉为了让自己暖和起来而产生的颤抖动作。

text: In fact, how we experience the cold is probably a combination of everything we've discussed,
zh: [46] 事实上,我们对寒冷的感受,可能是我们讨论过的所有因素的综合结果,

text: including genetic adaptations passed on from parents to children.
zh: [47] 包括从父母传给孩子的基因适应性。

text: Right, I'm off to find my gloves and woolly hat, so why don't you reveal the answer to the question, Phil?
zh: [48] 好了,我要去找我的手套和毛线帽了,Phil,不如你来揭晓那个问题的答案吧?

text: Yes, I asked where the lowest temperature on Earth was ever recorded.
zh: [49] 好的,我问的是,地球上有记录以来的最低气温是在哪里测得的。

text: You said B, Antarctica. And that is the right answer.
zh: [50] 你说的是B,南极洲。这就是正确答案。

text: The lowest temperature ever recorded on Earth was at the Vostok Research Station in Antarctica in 1983.
zh: [51] 地球上有记录以来的最低气温,是1983年在南极洲的沃斯托克科考站测得的。

text: Let's recap the vocabulary we've learnt, starting with the phrase first and foremost, meaning more than anything else.
zh: [52] 让我们来回顾一下今天学到的词汇,首先是短语“first and foremost”,意思是“最重要的是”。

text: A person who is tolerant of something is able to endure it without being hurt.
zh: [53] 对某事“tolerant”(耐受)的人,能够忍受它而不受伤害。

text: If you're used to something, you're familiar with it.
zh: [54] 如果你“used to”某事,就是说你对它很熟悉。

text: The idiom yes and no means partly and partly not, and is used when you can't give a clear answer to a question.
zh: [55] 习语“yes and no”意思是“部分是,部分不是”,用于当你无法对一个问题给出明确答案时。

text: The adverb subjectively means in a way that's based on your personal experience, rather than objective facts.
zh: [56] 副词“subjectively”(主观地)意思是基于个人体验,而不是客观事实。

text: And finally, a shiver is the shaking movement made by your muscles when you feel cold or afraid.
zh: [57] 最后,“shiver”(寒战)是指你感到寒冷或害怕时,肌肉产生的颤抖动作。

text: Once again, our six minutes are up. But remember, you'll find a quiz and a worksheet for this episode
zh: [58] 我们的六分钟又一次结束了。不过别忘了,你可以找到本期的测验和练习题,

text: on our website bbclearningenglish .com.
zh: [59] 就在我们的网站bbclearningenglish.com上。

text: See you there soon, but for now, it's goodbye. Bye.
zh: [60] 我们网站上很快见,不过现在,再见啦。拜拜。
